home.subtitle: A Digital Memorial
home.title: The Descent
home.description: From the ashes of World War I to the liberation of the concentration camps. A journey through the darkest chapter in human history.

phases.label: Phase
phases.desc1: Economic collapse, political instability, and the rise of extremism
phases.desc2: Systematic stripping of rights, persecution, and the machinery of oppression
phases.desc3: The industrial murder of six million Jews and millions of others
phases.desc4: The fall of the Nazi regime, liberation of survivors, and pursuit of justice

map.title: Geography of Genocide
map.subtitle: The network of camps across Nazi-occupied Europe, 1933-1945
map.mapCaption: Click on any camp marker for detailed information. Zoom and pan to explore.
map.deathTollNote: people were murdered at {name}. Each number represents a human life—someone's parent, child, sibling, friend.

footer.credits: Historical photographs courtesy of USHMM, Imperial War Museum, and Wikimedia Commons (public domain)

campDescriptions.auschwitz: The largest Nazi death camp. Over 1.1 million people murdered, approximately 90% of them Jews. Site of industrial-scale genocide using gas chambers.
campDescriptions.treblinka: Second deadliest camp. Almost all arrivals were gassed within hours. Destroyed by Nazis to hide evidence after prisoner uprising.
campDescriptions.belzec: First Operation Reinhard camp. Only 7 known survivors. Completely dismantled by Nazis to destroy evidence.
campDescriptions.sobibor: Site of the only successful mass escape. 300 prisoners broke out in October 1943, leading to camp closure.
campDescriptions.chelmno: First Nazi extermination camp. Used mobile gas vans. Only 4 known survivors.
campDescriptions.majdanek: First major camp liberated by Allies. Evidence largely intact as Nazis fled too quickly to destroy it.
campDescriptions.dachau: First Nazi concentration camp. Model for all subsequent camps. Medical experiments conducted on prisoners.
campDescriptions.buchenwald: One of the largest camps in Germany. Prisoners organized underground resistance. Elie Wiesel was imprisoned here.
campDescriptions.bergen-belsen: Where Anne Frank died. 14,000 more died after liberation despite medical care due to disease and starvation.
campDescriptions.sachsenhausen: Near Berlin. Served as training center for SS concentration camp officers. Used for experiments.
campDescriptions.ravensbruck: Largest camp for women. Medical experiments performed on female prisoners. Over 130,000 women imprisoned.
campDescriptions.mauthausen: Only camp classified "Grade III" (harshest). Infamous "Stairs of Death" at quarry. Spanish Republicans among victims.
campDescriptions.neuengamme: Largest camp in Northwest Germany. Over 80 subcamps. Many prisoners died in evacuations.
campDescriptions.flossenburg: Dietrich Bonhoeffer executed here. Quarry labor camp with brutal conditions.
campDescriptions.stutthof: First camp outside Germany. Last camp liberated. Gas chamber built in 1944.
campDescriptions.gross-rosen: Over 100 subcamps. Granite quarry. Many prisoners evacuated on death marches.
campDescriptions.natzweiler: Only concentration camp on French territory. Used for medical experiments. Gas chamber built for experiments.
campDescriptions.theresienstadt: Nazi propaganda camp shown to Red Cross. 140,000 Jews held here; 90,000 deported to death camps. Rich cultural life organized by prisoners.
campDescriptions.westerbork: Main Dutch transit camp. Anne Frank and family deported from here. 107,000 Jews sent to death camps.
campDescriptions.drancy: Main French transit camp. 65,000 Jews deported to death camps. Located in Parisian suburb.
campDescriptions.mechelen: Main Belgian transit camp. 25,000 Jews and 350 Roma deported. Only 1,200 survived the war.
campDescriptions.fossoli: Main Italian transit camp. Primo Levi deported from here. 5,000 Jews sent to Auschwitz.
campDescriptions.monowitz: IG Farben synthetic rubber plant. Worked prisoners to death. Part of Auschwitz complex.
campDescriptions.dora-mittelbau: Underground V-2 rocket factory. Prisoners worked in tunnels in horrific conditions.
campDescriptions.plaszow: Depicted in Schindler's List. Commandant Amon Göth executed prisoners for sport.
campDescriptions.janowska: Combined labor and extermination camp. Mass executions in nearby ravine.

closing.quote: Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it.
closing.p1: The Holocaust was not inevitable. At every step, choices were made—by leaders, by institutions, by ordinary people. Understanding how it happened is the first step in ensuring it never happens again.
closing.p2: They stopped it then. People fought, bled, and died to end the genocide. The Nuremberg principles established that some crimes are so terrible that no order justifies them, no government shields the guilty.
closing.p3: We remember the six million Jews and millions of others who were murdered. We honor the survivors who shared their stories. We honor those who liberated them. We commit to vigilance against hatred, discrimination, and the forces that enable genocide.
